title: Urge As Tax
html: Stub. Yahir's teen-era working theory that both giving in to urges AND suppressing them carry a cost — neither move is free. First articulated in March 2019 as the contradictory two-line aphorism "Those who can suppress the urges lose / Those who can't control their urges will lose." Read literally, the aphorism is internally contradictory; read symptomatically, it captures a 16-year-old discovering that attention itself is the tax — every urge eats compute whether you act on it or not. Adjacent to no runway discipline (which says discipline pays) and fear of not doing (which says inaction costs). The trio together = Yahir's discipline triangle.

related.label: Those with urges tend to lose — earliest impulse-control aphorism
You're ready, but know you can make mistakes — title-as-permission-slip
